speaker: interviewer
text: What happened next, Bent?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my wife, Marget, came, and of course, my friend I had met in Copenhagen. His name was Johnny Stompanato. He got me a job in Los Angeles. And so things were going pretty smooth until at-- I went out with Johnny and his girlfriend one night. It was Lana Turner. We had a good time. We were at that Crescendo night club. And the next day, I wrote in a newspaper that [? Walter Wilshon ?] had seen us at the-- it said Johnny Stompanato was seen with Lana Turner and an unknown couple. And the unknown couple was, of course, me. And then later on, Lana Turner's daughter killed my friend, Johnny Stompanato. Well, things weren't that good. Because I got my in-laws over and things took kinda a bad turn. And we had now two children. That was Debbie and Bent Herman, Junior. And my in-laws interfered all the time in our marriage. And finally, we got a separation, my my wife Marget and I. And she moved into Palms in Los Angeles, which were against what the judge had ordered. And I came every Sunday. And I had my children for a visit.

speaker: interviewee
text: All of a sudden, I came in to visit them one Sunday. And the apartment was empty. And I got kind of shocked. And I asked-- I went to the landlord and asked the landlord, where is the Blackmans? And he said, oh, didn't you know that Thursday, they all left for Denmark?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I called the Danish embassy. And I called around and found out that she had falsely written the children's name in her passport. And she smuggled them out the United States. Because it was against the judge's rule.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what could I do? Nothing. So I called my brother in Denmark. And he said, take it easy. Don't come running right after them. Because they are in hiding. So, so just to say that-- I'll go on. But for nine years, I didn't see my children. They hid them from my mother. My ex-wife went to my mother in Denmark and said, I want some money from you. Otherwise, you won't see the kids. So my mother said, you're not going to get any money. I haven't seen your kids up to now, so do whatever you think you have to do. And my mother, till she passed away, she never saw the kids.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, I lived on here in Los Angeles. I had lived in Encino. And then I had bought a home in, in Simi Valley. But of course, that was back in 1960.

speaker: interviewee
text: So at that time, I worked in Van Nuys. So it was long to drive. So I sold the house again because they had stolen everything out of the house there was. The only thing I had was my bed and my clothes. The rest they had taken, all our wedding gifts, everything. I never saw anything of it ever again. So-- Who's they?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my ex-wife, Marget, her mother and father, and some friends they had to help emptying the house when I were out Saturday night. Think about coming home from a party. And then your whole house is like an empty stick. Anyway, life went on. I sold the house. And I moved into Canoga Park, that I had gotten a new job for Litton Industry. And part of Litton Industry were located in Canoga Park.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in 1963, my secretary at that time at Litton wanted me to come to a party. She belong to a temple [INAUDIBLE] in Canoga Park. And she was a president of now the youth, adult club for the [INAUDIBLE]. And she said she was going to be installed as president. And she said if I wouldn't come to the installation. I said no, I, I don't want to go and look at wallflowers, so forget it, you know? But she kept pushing me and pushing me. And finally, she said you get-- buy a ticket for $5. That includes some hors d'oeuvres and include two drinks. I figure what, what-- for two drinks, what can I go wrong? So I bought a ticket. And I came there. And right away I said, oh, now I know why I'm sitting here at a bar and having a drink. But here come two ladies. They were both teachers with a third person, you know, which I hadn't even seen. And of course my friend introduced me and said, this is Gracie Haywem. She's also a foreigner, like you. So I said, oh, that's a nice way to put it, you know? But anyway, I said hi, nice to meet you, Gracie. And shall we sit down at the table, you know? And so we sat down and talked that night. And I thought, this one, I like, you know? Where was Gracie from?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gracie was born in Bombay, India. They left Bombay, India, in 1959, I think, because it kept the British independence, you know, and went to London. And then in London, she was there till 1963, when she came to United States to help her sister, Sophie Carver, which lived in Canoga Park also. And she wasn't too well at one point, so Gracie came to help her. And stayed with her. That's how-- and she came. They grew up in Bombay. She got a education in Bombay. She was a teacher. She had two other sisters, Sophie and Wilma, and three brothers, Benji, [? Abe, ?] and [? Ellis. ?] They all had gone to England. And some came to New York afterwards. But anyway, I proposed to Gracie after three months. And she said yes. And then her family started the-- so it took a little longer. But after a year, we got married on the 3rd of May, 1964. We waited a little bit to go on our honeymoon, because I had worked in the Danish organizations. And we had arranged a trip to Denmark. And so during the summer, we went to-- first to New York. And there, we went to the World's Fair in 1964 and saw the World's Fair. And the next day, we took off for Denmark. And we went and visit my family. And afterwards, we went to England and visit Gracie's family. And then afterwards, we came back. And we settled down in my apartment in Canoga Park. And a year later, we bought a home, also in Canoga Park, on Woodlake Avenue. And we moved in. And shortly thereafter that we had moved in, we fixed up the house. And it was real neat and nice. And we had our first baby in the 14 of June, of 1965. And that is Birgette. And then a year later, or 13 months later, we had our daughter. She was born also on the 14, but of July, of 1966. That's Tina. And so I work for Litton Industry. And we had quite the happy life there and enjoyed ourself. And later on, when we had-- in 1967-- no, in the '70s. In '77, we moved to Simi Valley because of busing problems and all. And I had gone to so many things. So I figured it was better to. And I were also in real estate business out in Simi. So I figured that would be a nice place to, to move to. And this was the house that we saw. Because we sit up on the hillside. And when it's not raining, then you can look over the whole valley and see all the mountains there surround us all the way around. And so we still live here, because the house were kind of demolished a little bit when during the earthquake. But we are back in shape. And so I'm sitting here and talking to you. We have been involved in many different organization through times and the Danish clubs. And, and we have gone to [INAUDIBLE]. That was where Gracie and I got married by a Rabbi [? Chohet ?]. We have held contact. I became member of an organization called Friends of Scandinavia, which really came out of-- the big organization is in New York with Mr. [? Neder. ?] And Victor Borge started a group that is called Thanks to Scandinavia. But ours became Friends of Scandinavia. And what we do is we help young students that come over from Denmark that study at the universities here in California. And that's one of my pet organization. And I go and speak to groups around, to temple schools, churches, whatever. And I relay the story about the flights. And how the Danes helped their fellow man and et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I also now am-- have helped for the last 10 years. But now, I'm on the board of directors of a Danish immigrant museum, which the museum is placed in Elkhorn, Iowa. Because that's the center of all Danes came from Denmark from the middle of the 1700s and up to 1800s, and now even in the 1990s.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called Across Ocean, Across Time. And that is one of the most beautiful places I have ever seen outside of Denmark. And that's where they placed the museum.

speaker: interviewee
text: The two things that my mother took from Germany and brought them back to Denmark-- I asked on 1956 what she was going to do with them. She said, I don't know. She said, you want him? And I said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And for 40 years, I have kept them. And here, a couple of weeks ago, we had a, a general board meeting in Solvang, which is the Danish capital of California. We had a board meeting where our board members come from all over United States. And I asked the president if they-- we have a Holocaust exhibit at this Danish museum, which is very befitting because Victor Borge has been involved. And many other Danish Jews also have been involved in the museum.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I thought that I would donate the book and the shawl from this preacher. And that could be hanging at the Danish museum or be shown with the other Holocaust exhibits that they have. And so I said that I would love to give them to the museum, because I thought that would be a beautiful resting place for those two items.

speaker: interviewee
text: And of course, I didn't think it was such a great thing. Everybody stood up and applauded and thought it was absolutely fantastic. I will get some pictures later of it that were taken that day. But I thought that it was such a neat idea. And I knew my mother would like it. So I did it. And I said to Gracie, it's, it's so nice to know that now, a lot of other people will have the time to see them. And of course, like I say, if I can say it now that I'm sitting and speak, I think that Steven Spielberg is doing such a fabulous job here. And I saw his movie. And I were not a bit-- like so many were uncomfortable. Because I have gone through it. So I know at what was happening there was as authentic as you can get to it. And I just hope that Mr. Spielberg will come one day to the Danish museum, that he also know that, that many Danes throughout the United States have done much for the world. Like we had a Dane that fell at the Alamo. We had a Danish woman who [? said ?] during Second World War was a Dane, et cetera, et cetera.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I think that that is part of my story. And I hope and I say thank you for having the opportunity to speak on the tape. And I would like to say it, [DANISH SPEECH]. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE] No.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just let her do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: I would like to introduce Bent Lerno and his wife.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd like to introduce my wife. This is Gracie Haywem Lerno. We got married the 3rd of May, 1964. And we are soon coming up on our 32nd wedding anniversary. We are the one that have two children. My oldest daughter is-- they say here, Bridgett, but it is in Danish, it's Birgette, like beer and gitta. So it's Birgette. And our second daughter is Tina. And Tina lives in Hollywood. And she works for a company called The Seven Level which make CD-ROMs. She is a color coordinator when they make CDs. And my other daughter, Birgette, lives in San Diego, is married, and live in San Diego. And they have a nice condo. And she works for-- I don't know what the company's called. I'm telling you. She just started there for-- but she's the inboard-- it's a worldwide corporation. She's the inboard travel agent. And she is super. And she is sports-like-- golf, tennis, swimming. She has always been. Tina hated all kinds of sports. So it's a, a whole different world with those two. But Gracie and I have had a nice life together. And we have, we think, of a beautiful home, especially after we, we rebuilt it. Whatever you see on all the walls in the house as you're here today is, everything is new. Because everything came down. I like-- and Gracie, too-- we like to collect art and artwork and art pieces. And we have done very well with it.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my brother in Denmark that got me interested in modern art. He has probably, private collection in Denmark, one of the biggest modern art collections at his house. And so I took from it. And some of my sculptures here and all, we have dragged them from Denmark here, you know? And-- but we just enjoyed it and, and enjoyed our kids and our life. So-- and Gracie, I, I don't want to advertise for her, but she works for a big airline and is a flight attendant now, has been for the last 20 years or right there about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just about. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I'm retired. And I do a lot of work for, like I say, for different organizations. We also have, of course, an organization which is called Rebild Society. That's an organization with-- that celebrates the 4th of July with United States. And every year in Denmark in the city of Rebild, we celebrate 4th of July. And about 10,000 to 20,000 people come there every year.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you like to say something, Gracie?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'd love to. I just want to say it's been very exciting being married to Bent. It's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us a little bit about your, your mom and dad and where they came from.

speaker: interviewee
text: Okay. I met Bent 33 years ago in a-- he was at the synagogue.

speaker: interviewee
text: I already told that [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: Okay. And I'm originally, originally from Bombay, India. And my mother came from Baghdad and met my father. It was a match, arranged marriage, like they used to do in those days. In India, we have about a million Indian Jews And so I'm very proud to be part of that community. My grandmother and grandfather had to escape Iraq and flee to Israel over land and water to get there. So that's another story that they had to go through. And it was very exciting to meet my grandmother for just once in my lifetime.

words.text: to be part of that community.

speaker: interviewee
text: Your father worked.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father was the first Jewish lawyer in India. And his family was very well-known, the Haywem family. His sister was the first Jewish nurse. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: Of course, what is your mother's background? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother's name was Emma [INAUDIBLE] Sassoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you very much, both of you. And now, we'll continue with the pictures.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: The picture you see right now is my grandmother on my mother's side. Her name is Johanna Lowenberg Solamon. She was born in 1854. And she lived till 1904, so I have never seen her.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in Copenhagen. This is my grandfather on my mother's side. His name is Jacob Hartwig Solamon. And the picture was taken in Copenhagen in the beginning of the 1900s. This appeared in a local newspaper Simi Valley, It was my story about immigrant fleeing the Nazi. In the background, you're seeing my mom and my dad, It is Martha Lerno and Herman Lerno. And the front is myself, Bent Lerno. This picture was taken in 1928 in Charlottenlund, Denmark. The two that are standing in the background-- on the left is my father, Herman Lerno. On the right is my brother, Harry Lerno. Seated starting from the left is my brother Richard, the oldest. Then the next one on his lap is myself, Bent Lerno. The third one from the left is my brother, Paul Lerno. And seated next to Paul is my sister, Ida. And the last one is my mother, Martha Lerno. This was taken in Charlottenlund, Denmark. This picture was taken in 1945 in July. That was the Danish brigade, or Danforce. We were lying down in the southern part of Denmark between the border between Denmark and Germany. And that picture was taken when I were on guard duty. This is Bent Lerno. This is also myself, Bent Lerno. this is taken in 1946 in Copenhagen. This picture was taken of Gracie Lerno and myself in 1964, the 3rd of May, when we got married in Temple [? Betgotish ?] in Canoga Park, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in California, Simi Valley-- no, I should say, Canoga Park, California. This is my two daughters I have with Gracie, which you just saw the wedding picture. On the left is Tina. And on the right is Birgette. That was on her wedding day in 1991. This is my oldest grandson, Simon Miara. He is my daughter in Denmark, Debbie Miara. And he will be, this year, 15 years old. And this picture is of my granddaughter Natali, also daughter of Debbie Miara in Copenhagen, Denmark.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old is she?

speaker: interviewee
text: She is 13 years old. This is my twin grandchildren, also children of Debbie Miara, in Copenhagen, Denmark. From the left, you see Denise. And on the right is David. They are eight years of age today. This picture you see now was taken on the 5th of May, 1995. It is the 50th anniversary of the Danish military group. And both my brother Paul and I belonged to the Danforce. And we were home celebrating the liberation 50 years later. The background is a Danish palace that's under repair. But you see the split Danish flag-- so 5th of May, 1995, Copenhagen, Denmark. Thank you.
